analogy: Think of "Biotechnology Principles and Processes" through the chapter's own opening images: Ever since the days of Rene Descartes, the French philosopher, mathematician and biologist of seventeenth century, all human knowledge especially natural sciences were directed to develop technologies which add to the creature comforts of human lives, as also value to human life. The whole approach... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: HERBERT BOYER, 9.1 PRINCIPLES OF BIOTECHNOLOGY, CHAPTER 9, BIOTECHNOLOGY : PRINCIPLES. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Biotechnology Principles and Processes is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that ever since the days of Rene Descartes, the French philosopher, mathematician and biologist of seventeenth century, all human knowledge especially natural sciences were directed to develop technologies which add to the creature comforts of human lives, as also value to human life.

The text explains that the whole approach to understanding natural phenomena became anthropocentric.

The text explains that physics and chemistry gave rise to engineering, technologies and industries which all worked for human comfort and welfare.

The text explains that the major utility of the biological world is as a source of food.

The text explains that biotechnology, the twentieth century off-shoot of modern biology, changed our daily life as its products brought qualitative improvement in health and food production.

The text explains that the basic principles underlying biotechnological processes and some applications are highlighted and discussed in this unit.

The text explains that chapter 9 Biotechnology : Principles and Processes Chapter 10 Biotechnology and Its Applications

The text explains that herbert Boyer was born in 1936 and brought up in a corner of western Pennsylvania where railroads and mines were the destiny of most young men.

The text explains that he completed graduate work at the University of Pittsburgh, in 1963, followed by three years of post-graduate studies at Yale.

The text explains that in 1966, Boyer took over assistant professorship at the University of California at San Francisco.

The text explains that by 1969, he performed studies on a couple of restriction enzymes of the E. coli bacterium with especially useful properties.

The text explains that boyer observed that these enzymes have the capability of cutting DNA strands in a particular fashion, which left what has became known as ‘sticky ends’ on the strands.

The text explains that these clipped ends made pasting together pieces of DNA a precise exercise.

The text explains that this discovery, in turn, led to a rich and rewarding conversation in Hawaii with a Stanford scientist named Stanley Cohen.

The text explains that cohen had been studying small ringlets of DNA called plasmids and which float about freely in the cytoplasm of certain bacterial cells and replicate independently from the coding strand of DNA.

The text explains that cohen had developed a method of removing these plasmids from the cell and then reinserting them in other cells.

The text explains that combining this process with that of DNA splicing enabled Boyer and Cohen to recombine segments of DNA in desired configurations and insert the DNA in bacterial cells, which could then act as manufacturing plants for specific proteins.

The text explains that biotechnology deals with techniques of using live organisms or enzymes from organisms to produce products and processes useful to humans.

The text explains that in this sense, making curd, bread or wine, which are all microbe-mediated processes, could also be thought as a form of biotechnology.

The text explains that however, it is used in a restricted sense today, to refer to such of those processes which use genetically modified organisms to achieve the same on a larger scale.

The text explains that further, many other processes/techniques are also included under biotechnology.

The text explains that 9.1 PRINCIPLES OF BIOTECHNOLOGY Among many, the two core techniques that enabled birth of modern biotechnology are : (i) Genetic engineering : Techniques to alter the chemistry of genetic material (DNA and RNA), CHAPTER 9 BIOTECHNOLOGY : PRINCIPLES AND PROCESSES 9.1 Principles of Biotechnology 9.2 Tools of Recombinant DNA Technology 9.3 Processes of Recombinant DNA Technology

The text explains that (ii) Bioprocess engineering : Maintenance of sterile (microbial contamination-free) ambience in chemical engineering processes to enable growth of only the desired microbe/eukaryotic cell in large quantities for the manufacture of biotechnological products like antibiotics, vaccines, enzymes, etc.

The text explains that bIOTECHNOLOGY : PRINCIPLES AND PROCESSES 165 ‘molecular scissors’– restriction enzymes.

The text explains that as against a word-palindrome where the same word is read in both directions, the palindrome in DNA is a sequence of base pairs that reads same on the two strands when orientation of

The text explains that bIOTECHNOLOGY : PRINCIPLES AND PROCESSES 167 reading is kept the same.

The text explains that for example, the following sequences reads the same on the two strands in 5' à 3' direction.

The text explains that this is also true if read in the 3' à 5' direction.

The text explains that restriction endonucleases are used in genetic engineering to form ‘recombinant’ molecules of DNA, which are composed of DNA from different sources/genomes.

The text explains that 168 BIOLOGY You may have realised that normally, unless one cuts the vector and the source DNA with the same restriction enzyme, the recombinant vector molecule cannot be created.

The text explains that you can see bright orange coloured bands of DNA in a ethidium bromide stained gel exposed to UV light (Figure 9.3).

The text explains that bIOTECHNOLOGY : PRINCIPLES AND PROCESSES 169 The following are the features that are required to facilitate cloning into a vector.

The text explains that transformation is a procedure through which a piece of DNA is introduced in a host bacterium (you will study the process in subsequent section).

The text explains that for example, Agrobacterium tumifaciens, a pathogen of several dicot plants is able to deliver a piece of DNA known as ‘T-DNA’ to transform normal plant cells into a tumor and direct these tumor cells to produce the chemicals required by the pathogen.

The text explains that bIOTECHNOLOGY : PRINCIPLES AND PROCESSES 171 the bacterium through pores in its cell wall.

The text explains that in a method known as micro-injection, recombinant DNA is directly injected into the nucleus of an animal cell.

The text explains that now that we have learnt about the tools for constructing recombinant DNA, let us discuss the processes facilitating recombinant DNA technology.

The text explains that 9.3 PROCESSES OF RECOMBINANT DNA TECHNOLOGY Recombinant DNA technology involves several steps in specific sequence such as isolation of DNA, fragmentation of DNA by restriction endonucleases, isolation of a desired DNA fragment, ligation of the DNA fragment into a vector, transferring the recombinant DNA into the host, culturing the host cells in a medium at large scale and extraction of the desired product.

The text explains that the process is repeated with the vector DNA also.

The text explains that the joining of DNA involves several processes.

The text explains that after having cut the source DNA as well as the vector DNA with a specific restriction enzyme, the cut out ‘gene of interest’ from the source DNA and the cut vector with space are mixed and ligase is added.

The text explains that bIOTECHNOLOGY : PRINCIPLES AND PROCESSES 173 sets of primers (small chemically synthesised oligonucleotides that are complementary to the regions of DNA) and the enzyme DNA polymerase.

The text explains that if the process of replication of DNA is repeated many times, the segment of DNA can be amplified to approximately billion times, i.e., 1 billion copies are made.

The text explains that such repeated amplification is achieved by the use of a thermostable DNA polymerase (isolated from a bacterium, Thermus aquaticus), which remain active during the high temperature induced denaturation of double stranded DNA.

The text explains that can you think of any reason why there is a need for large-scale production?

The text explains that the cells can also be multiplied in a continuous culture system wherein the used medium is drained out from one side while fresh medium is added from the other to maintain the cells in their physiologically most

The text explains that if you look at the figure closely you will see that the bioreactor has an agitator system, an oxygen delivery system and a foam control system, a temperature control system, pH control system and sampling ports so that small volumes of the culture can be withdrawn periodically.

The text explains that 9.3.6 Downstream Processing After completion of the biosynthetic stage, the product has to be subjected through a series of processes before it is ready for marketing as a finished active log/exponential phase.

The text explains that can you recall meiosis and indicate at what stage a recombinant DNA is made?

The text explains that can you think and answer how a reporter enzyme can be used to monitor transformation of host cells by foreign DNA in addition to a selectable marker? product.

The text explains that the processes include separation and purification, which are collectively referred to as downstream processing.

The text explains that the product has to be formulated with suitable preservatives.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 9: Biotechnology Principles and Processes

prelims_traps: Do not treat HERBERT BOYER and 9.1 PRINCIPLES OF BIOTECHNOLOGY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Biotechnology Principles and Processes; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Biotechnology Principles and Processes through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use HERBERT BOYER and 9.1 PRINCIPLES OF BIOTECHNOLOGY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
